narrative:
Short impact summary

Published in 2023 by Jeremy Waddington, Torsten Müller, and Kurt Vandaele, Trade Unions in the European Union has established itself as a notable reference in the field of European industrial relations, accumulating 32 citations and nearly 2,400 open-access downloads since publication. Its citation performance places it in the top 2% of comparable works, and it has attracted scholarly attention across more than a dozen countries and disciplines.

Full narrative

As volume 86 in Peter Lang's long-running Travail et Société / Work and Society series, this book arrives in distinguished company — a series that has addressed European labour market policy, pension reform, and workplace diversity over several decades. Waddington, Müller, and Vandaele bring together expertise in political economy and European integration to offer a systematic account of trade union structures, strategies, and challenges across EU member states. The open-access availability of the volume through OAPEN has clearly amplified its reach: 2,384 downloads represent a substantial level of active engagement for a specialist scholarly monograph, with particularly strong uptake recorded in the spring months following publication.

The book's citation record reflects both speed and breadth of scholarly uptake. With 11 citing works appearing in 2024 and 17 in 2025, the trajectory is steeply upward rather than front-loaded. The citing literature spans an impressively wide range of venues — from Transfer: European Review of Labour and Research, where it has been cited six times, to the British Journal of Industrial Relations, Democratization, Social Policy and Administration, and even Safety Science. This breadth signals that the volume's arguments are being mobilised not only by labour relations specialists but also by scholars working on welfare state politics, democratic backsliding, occupational health, and comparative political economy. The geographic diversity of citing authors — spanning Belgium, Poland, Sweden, Germany, Italy, Ireland, and beyond — underscores the genuinely pan-European relevance of the work.

The institutional landscape of citing scholars is noteworthy. The European Trade Union Institute leads among citing institutions, which is consistent with the book's policy-oriented scope, but engagement also comes from university-based researchers at the Stockholm School of Economics, SGH Warsaw School of Economics, the University of Silesia in Katowice, and University College Dublin, among others. This mix of policy research institutes and academic departments suggests the volume is functioning simultaneously as a reference work for practitioners and an anchoring text for scholarly debate. The book also appears on four English-language Wikipedia pages — covering topics such as workers' initiatives, trade union relations with a major technology firm, and the contemporary history of Luxembourg — indicating that its authority is being invoked in open encyclopaedic contexts as well.

Situated within a broader conversation about convergence and divergence in European industrial relations, the book is being cited in debates about neoliberal convergence hypotheses, union strategies in the platform economy, and the relationship between organised labour and democratic resilience. That such varied threads of inquiry reach back to a single volume speaks to the synthetic ambition of the authors' framework. Taken together, the download figures, citation velocity, geographic spread, and cross-disciplinary uptake mark Trade Unions in the European Union as a work that is actively shaping scholarly and policy discourse on organised labour in Europe.
